title: Periodontal Staging and Grading Diagnostic Tool
subtitle: Based on the 2018 International Classification of Periodontal and Peri-implant Diseases

meta.description: Periodontal staging and grading diagnostic tool, based on the 2018 International Classification of Periodontal and Peri-implant Diseases, originally developed by Huan Liu from Wuhan University School and Hospital of Stomatology.

results.title: Diagnosis Results
results.stage3_desc: Severe periodontitis: CAL ≥5mm, bone loss >30%, possible furcation involvement
results.stage4_desc: Advanced periodontitis: In addition to Stage III features, tooth loss or mobility
results.gradeA_desc: Slow progression: bone loss <0.5mm in 5 years
results.gradeB_desc: Moderate progression: bone loss 0.5-1.0mm in 5 years
results.gradeC_desc: Rapid progression: bone loss >1.0mm in 5 years

flowchart.title: Periodontal Staging and Grading Flowchart
flowchart.svg_not_supported: Your browser does not support SVG images

about.title: About Periodontal Staging and Grading System
about.intro: In 2018, the World Workshop on the Classification of Periodontal and Peri-implant Diseases and Conditions released a new classification system for periodontal and peri-implant diseases. This system introduced the concepts of staging and grading for periodontitis to more comprehensively assess disease severity, complexity, and progression risk.

staging.title: Staging
staging.description: Staging primarily reflects disease severity and complexity, divided into four stages I, II, III, and IV:

stage3.title: Stage III
stage3.desc: Severe periodontitis, CAL ≥5mm, bone loss >30%, tooth loss ≤4 due to periodontitis

stage4.title: Stage IV
stage4.desc: Advanced periodontitis, CAL ≥5mm, bone loss >30%, tooth loss >4 due to periodontitis, may require complex rehabilitation

grading.title: Grading
grading.description: Grading primarily reflects disease progression rate and risk factors, divided into three grades A, B, and C:
grading.factors: Grading is primarily based on evidence of disease progression over the past 5 years or bone loss/age ratio, considering risk factors such as smoking and diabetes.
